title: Other Specialized Design Services

narrative.executive_view: Other Specialized Design Services is too heterogeneous to underwrite as one clean roll-up. I score fashion and accessory design as the hypothesized dominant fee subsegment, while treating jewelry, textile, footwear, costume and fabrication-heavy exhibit/sample work as a heterogeneous tail. In that dominant workflow, AI already crosses the timing threshold but does not eliminate the physical handoff to materials, fit and production. The deal screen fails because the available evidence does not establish transferable fee books or a code-specific buyer universe, not because every specialized-design task is instantly automated.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Fashion-design work mixes idea generation with production reality. BLS lists trend study, collection themes, CAD design, fabric selection, prototypes, presentations and final-production oversight as normal duties [S2]. Marketplace listings similarly bundle mood boards, flats, tech packs and sourcing with manufacturer collaboration [S4]. Generative AI and design software can accelerate sketches, variants, mood boards, early visualizations and documentation; physical sampling, fit correction, material choice, factory communication and approval retain a human-and-physical loop.
narrative.value_capture: Captured savings should be modest. The external market exposes comparable freelancers worldwide and commonly packages a garment, tech pack, collection or launch as a defined project; Upwork lists fashion-design work at roughly $20–$50 per hour and discrete project budgets [S4]. This supports some fee retention for named taste, client context and production know-how, but clients can re-scope work when first-draft visual and technical material becomes cheaper. The savings channel is distinct from terminal demand: a studio may keep a fabrication or sourcing engagement while losing design hours and price per deliverable.
narrative.adoption_timing: I set current effective adoption to 51.7% and t50 to zero. The direct source is not a 541490 census: SNAAP's Fall 2025 U.S. survey of more than 2,000 arts-and-design alumni found 51.7% used generative AI professionally, with productivity, ideation and editing as common motivations [S1]. Fashion-specific executive evidence is directionally consistent but lower and broader: more than 35% of fashion and luxury executives report using generative AI in selected functions [S3]. The threshold conclusion is therefore low-confidence and applies to workflow use, not to full physical-product substitution.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The supplied target band contains only 82 firms and the total establishment population is heavily micro-firmed, making named-designer, client and factory relationships central portability risks. Broad fashion M&A evidence is not a substitute: current transactions center on brands, retail scale, licensing and portfolios rather than independent specialized-design-service studios [S6]. I count no verified active consolidators for this target and use stressed illustrative entry/exit assumptions. Generic small-business succession evidence can support a broad owner-age proxy, but it cannot create a code-specific supply pipeline [S5].
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED rather than MELTING. In the dominant fashion-design hypothesis, paid external work survives where a studio converts a concept into a manufacturable garment: selection of fabrics and trims, prototype and fit feedback, sourcing, production files and the client/factory relationship matter [S2][S4]. But pure visual concepts, pattern/color variants and first-draft specifications are directly exposed to AI-enabled in-house teams and global freelancers. Fabrication-heavy and sample-room niches are more durable; pure sketch, textile-motif and fee-only design niches are more vulnerable. That divergence is why the code remains heterogeneous.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainty is classification, not arithmetic. The immutable role mix is a 4-digit 5414 cell whose leading Interior and Graphic Designer rows belong principally to sibling codes; the likely core Fashion Designers and physical sample roles are in the unlisted residual. Fashion is only a hypothesis for the dominant revenue subsegment, and the research sources describe occupations, creative alumni, fashion brands or marketplace work rather than U.S. 541490 revenue. I therefore preserve the supplied shares, disclose the proxy, discount the contaminated rows, and treat both consolidation and valuation inputs as low-confidence stress assumptions.

sources.title: SNAAP, Adoption, Applications, and Attitudes Toward AI Among Arts and Design Alumni
sources.what_it_supports: U.S. Fall-2025 survey of more than 2,000 arts and design alumni: 51.7% reported professional generative-AI use, primarily as support for productivity, idea development and editing. Used as an adoption proxy, not a 541490 census.

sources.title: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Fashion Designers Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.what_it_supports: Fashion-designer duties include CAD, trend research, fabric selection, prototypes, presentations and final-production oversight; fashion designers work in design firms and across apparel/manufacturing contexts. Used to distinguish digital from physical work.

sources.title: McKinsey and The Business of Fashion, State of Fashion 2026
sources.what_it_supports: More than 35% of fashion and luxury executives report using generative AI in selected functions including image creation and product discovery; fashion companies are applying automation to routine work. It describes brands and fashion companies, not independent 541490 studios.

sources.title: Upwork, Best Freelance Fashion Designers for Hire
sources.what_it_supports: Fashion-design marketplace guide describing ideation, tech packs, sourcing, production coordination, hourly or milestone payment, and project budgets for garments, tech packs, collections and launches. Used for pricing structure and global freelance competition.

sources.title: U.S. Bank, 2025 Small Business Perspective Survey
sources.what_it_supports: National survey of 1,000 U.S. businesses with two to 99 employees and up to $25M revenue: more than half of owners are over 55 and only 54% report a succession plan. Used only as a general size-band owner-age proxy.

sources.title: Vogue, The State of Fashion M&A in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Fashion deal coverage centers on brand, retail, licensing and portfolio transactions, with conservative valuations amid macro pressure. Used to explain why fashion-brand M&A cannot be treated as evidence of a 541490 design-services roll-up market.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541490 ($2,027,827,545) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541490 ($3,671,109,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $308,134 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541490); class <5: 2,871 firms, avg revenue/firm $385,018 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,255 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 302 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,501,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,384,664 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $529,700 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,240,281 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,604 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,541,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,814,274 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,497,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,772,850 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 82
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: The model preserves the supplied 4-digit wage shares but treats the unlisted residual as the likely mix of specialized design, production coordination and some physical sample/fabrication work. Visual ideation, CAD and technical documentation are exposed; physical fitting, materials and production handoff curb total replaceability.
caveats: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5414 and is contaminated by sibling interior and graphic-design occupations.
Fashion is a hypothesis for the dominant 541490 revenue subsegment, not a verified code-level revenue split.
No source maps AI task exposure directly to the supplied wage shares.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Interior Designers is a sibling-code contaminant. Some concept visualization and documentation automate, but this row is not treated as representative of the target code.
Graphic Designers is another sibling-code contaminant; its digital-output exposure is recognized but discounted for target-code relevance.
Operations, schedules and drafts compress, while named-designer leadership, commercial responsibility and client ownership remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Pitch material and lead administration can automate, but licensing, client selling and relationship management are only partly exposed.
Project documentation, milestones and vendor follow-up are tool-friendly; physical sample review and escalation remain less automatable.
General studio-office work, records and routine coordination are highly automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical handling and movement are not directly replaced by generative AI.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research synthesis, trend-summary drafts and basic marketing analysis compress, while accountable positioning work remains.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, correspondence and records work are highly exposed to automation.
AI can accelerate concepts and versions, but senior selection, client communication and creative accountability remain significant.

The residual likely contains the missing specialized designers, technical design and some sample/fabrication roles. It is assigned partial—not near-total—exposure to reflect the physical production loop.

pi_dist.rationale: Global marketplace supply and defined project budgets make it difficult for a generic studio to retain all labor savings; distribution rests mainly in named-client relationships and production knowledge.
caveats: Marketplace work is a pricing and supply proxy, not a census of U.S. design-studio contracts.

pi_moat.rationale: Manufacturing constraints, materials, fit feedback and a studio's client/factory relationships create a narrow moat, but named taste and project files are often portable with the founder.
caveats: The assessment is for the hypothesized fashion-design dominant subsegment and does not establish a code-wide moat.

t50_years.rationale: SNAAP reports 51.7% professional GenAI use among U.S. arts-and-design alumni, and fashion executives report current deployment in selected functions; this supports current effective workflow adoption at or above 50%.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0 now; if 541490 adoption is below the proxy, 1–3 years
caveats: Neither source reports a U.S. 541490 establishment-level adoption rate.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Uses SNAAP's 51.7% observed professional-use rate as a broad arts-and-design proxy, corroborated directionally by fashion-company adoption evidence.
caveats: The direct 51.7% observation is not code-specific and includes arts/design alumni outside specialized design services.

historical_analogs.value: CAD, digital patternmaking and 3D visualization progressively moved early fashion-design work from manual drafting toward software; generative AI extends that compression into ideation and drafts while physical fit and manufacturing validation remain.
historical_analogs.rationale: Both sources document a workflow already centered on CAD, 3D and production specifications; the historical analogy is a reasoned comparison, not a measured price-series result.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Digital design stages compress faster than physical production stages
caveats: No 541490 historical price or margin series was located.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A conservative 30% estimate for owners aged 60+ in the target, below the generic report's statement that more than half of comparable small-business owners are over 55.
caveats: The source covers U.S. firms with two to 99 employees and up to $25M revenue, not 541490 or owners aged 60+ specifically.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: General succession-plan gaps are documented, but no evidence identifies a 541490-specific shortage of sellable studios or a successor-demand imbalance.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false unless a code-specific shortage is documented
caveats: Do not turn generic owner retirement pressure into a target-code roll-up supply claim.

active_consolidators.rationale: No active 541490 specialized-design-services consolidator was confirmed. The available fashion M&A source describes brands, retailers and licensing portfolios instead of a design-studio buyer set.
caveats: Absence of confirmed public evidence is not proof that no private buyer exists.

buy_mult.rationale: Illustrative stressed entry multiple for a founder-dependent services operator; fashion brand and retail transactions cannot be used as direct comparables.
caveats: No observed 541490 lower-middle-market transaction multiple was found.

exit_mult.rationale: Illustrative exit multiple below entry reflects AI-driven fee compression, key-person portability and no verified consolidator market for the service code.
caveats: This is a stress assumption, not a direct transaction observation; broad fashion brand M&A is intentionally excluded as a comp.

pricing_structure.value: Hourly or milestone/project fees for garments, tech packs, collections, launches and ongoing seasonal support
pricing_structure.rationale: Upwork states payment may be hourly or by milestone and lists project budgets for garment design, tech packs, collection work, launches and ongoing design support.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Hourly, milestone and project pricing; royalty arrangements are possible but not shown as dominant
caveats: Marketplace prices and billing modes may not represent every U.S. specialized design studio.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. High task exposure and adoption at the 50% boundary refer to digital workflow tasks; they do not imply immediate replacement of fitting, fabrication, sourcing or factory-facing work.

terminal_value.justification: The hypothesized dominant fashion-design service retains paid demand where it turns a concept into a manufacturable product, but its visual ideation and documentation layers are exposed. Physical-fabrication niches are more durable and pure design-fee niches weaker.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No fallback was invented for immutable dataset fields. The required 4-digit 5414 role mix is a material proxy for 541490 and its leading Interior and Graphic Designer rows are sibling-code contamination; the record preserves and discounts rather than replaces them.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Project and milestone packages invite clients to request the same deliverable for less when AI reduces drafts, variants and documentation time. Physical samples, fit sessions and factory handoff can preserve work but do not necessarily preserve the lost design-hour revenue.

dataset_mismatch.description: The immutable role mix is 4-digit 5414. Its top Interior and Graphic Designer rows belong principally to sibling codes, while likely core specialized-design roles such as Fashion Designers and physical sample workers sit in the residual.
sensitivity: The two contaminated rows are discounted rather than reweighted.
The residual is 37.39% of wage share and therefore drives most of the exposure uncertainty.
No dataset shares, counts or values are altered.

subsegments: Hypothesized dominant fashion and accessory design services
Jewelry, textile and footwear design services
Exhibit, float, sample-making and fabrication-adjacent work
Interior design (541410), graphic design (541430), industrial/product design (541420), apparel-brand employees and brand/product revenue excluded
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Fashion and accessory design services (hypothesis, not verified by a 541490 revenue split)
market_boundary.selection_basis: Frozen brief, BLS fashion-designer task description and marketplace evidence identify a plausible workflow, but no authoritative code-level subsegment revenue data was located.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 now; if code-specific adoption is below 50%, 1–3 years
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 10 at zero and falls to 10/t50 if the broad adoption proxy does not carry to 541490.
The missing core occupations and physical tail in the residual can move V substantially; the selected estimate is at the V cap.
Exit at or below entry produces M=0; no direct 541490 transaction set supports an expansion thesis.

reviewer_note: Fresh live sources were opened on 2026-07-21. The fashion-design thesis is a cautiously selected dominant-subsegment hypothesis, not a code-level revenue fact. The record deliberately excludes fashion-brand M&A as a valuation comparable for independent specialized-design services.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=0, A=10
